scenario: A public infrastructure promise needs community trust, transparent evidence, repair paths, and a record of what was actually promised versus assumed.
signal: A community-impact promise has entered the field.
invitation: Invite stakeholders to separate facts, promises, assumptions, and open questions.
affectedParties: Residents, local officials, developers, workers, nearby landowners, and future generations.
consent: Participation requires clear public notice, understandable records, and refusal or objection paths.
alignment: The process must preserve truth, dignity, accountability, and future repair.
action: Create a public receipt table and request source documents before treating claims as settled.
outcome: Trust improves only when claims become traceable receipts.
renewal: Update receipts after each meeting and mark unresolved claims as unknown or disputed.
evidence: Meeting notes, official filings, contracts, audit records, and public statements.
assumptions: Some promises may have been repeated without direct source evidence.
unknowns: Which claims have binding documents and which are aspirational only.
